mappings.rationale: Directly addresses the nature, scope, and methods of philosophy, which is the core subject of section A.
Section B.1 covers metaphysics or speculative philosophy including its history, nature, scope, and core problems.
Section B.6 covers ethics or moral philosophy including metaethics, normative ethics, and its relation to other disciplines.
Section B.7 covers political philosophy including its nature, scope, relation to political science, and history.
Section B.8 covers aesthetics including its nature, scope, development, problems, and relation to other disciplines.
Section B.2 covers the philosophy of nature and B.13 references the philosophy of science as a division of philosophy.
Section B.2.e specifically lists the philosophy of biology as part of the philosophy of nature.
Section B.2.d specifically lists the philosophy of physics as part of the philosophy of nature.
Section B.14 references the philosophy of religion as a division of philosophy.
Section B.15 references the philosophy of law as a division of philosophy.
Section B.4 covers the philosophy of mind or philosophical psychology including its history, nature, scope, and problems.
Section B.3 covers epistemology or theory of knowledge including its history, nature, scope, and problems.
Consciousness is central to the philosophy of mind (B.4) and its relation to empirical sciences and other philosophical disciplines.
Free will is a core problem in both the philosophy of mind (B.4.c) and ethics (B.6.c), two major divisions of philosophy.
Section B.1.c covers problems in metaphysics including questions about the nature of reality and being.
Causation is a core problem in metaphysics (B.1.c) and the philosophy of nature (B.2.c), two major divisions of philosophy.
Section B.10 references the philosophy of logic as a division of philosophy, and logic relates to epistemology and metaphysics.
Utilitarianism is a major normative ethical theory relevant to the types of ethics discussed in section B.6.a.
Virtue ethics is a major ethical tradition relevant to the types and problems of ethics discussed in section B.6.
Environmental ethics relates to the problems in ethics (B.6.c) and the relation of ethics to other branches of knowledge (B.6.b).
Animal rights relates to problems in ethics (B.6.c) and the philosophical anthropology of human nature versus animal nature (B.5).
Medical ethics is an applied area relating to the relation of ethics to other branches of knowledge (B.6.b).
The concept of evil connects to problems in ethics (B.6.c) and metaphysics (B.1.c), two major divisions of philosophy.
Love connects to problems in the philosophy of mind (B.4.c), ethics (B.6.c), and philosophical anthropology (B.5.c).
Beauty is a central problem in aesthetics (B.8.c) and the aesthetic experience (B.8.b).
The meaning of life connects to problems in philosophical anthropology (B.5.c), metaphysics (B.1.c), and ethics (B.6.c).
Conscience relates to problems in ethics (B.6.c) and the philosophy of mind (B.4.c), two major divisions of philosophy.
Toleration is a problem in political philosophy (B.7) and ethics (B.6), two major divisions of philosophy.

mappings.vsiAuthor: Emma Borg and Sarah A. Fisher
mappings.rationale: Section B.9 covers the philosophy of language, and meaning is its central problem connecting to logic and epistemology.
Objectivity is a core epistemological problem (B.3.c) and relates to the philosophy of science and criteria of truth (A.4).
Section A.2 covers diverse views of the methods of philosophy, which is directly the subject of this VSI.
Analytic philosophy represents a major school with distinctive methods (A.2) and conceptions of philosophy (A.1).
Continental philosophy represents an alternative conception of philosophy (A.1) with distinct methods (A.2) and forms of exposition (A.3).
Hermeneutics represents a philosophical method (A.2) and connects to epistemology (B.3) and the philosophy of language (B.9).
Feminist philosophy engages with multiple divisions of philosophy including ethics (B.6), political philosophy (B.7), and philosophical anthropology (B.5).
Time is a core problem in metaphysics (B.1.c) and the philosophy of nature (B.2.c).
Infinity connects to the philosophy of mathematics (B.11) and metaphysics (B.1.c) as divisions of philosophy.
Information relates to epistemology (B.3) and the philosophy of language (B.9) as divisions of philosophy.
The concept of nothing connects to metaphysical problems (B.1.c) about being, existence, and the nature of reality.
The existence and attributes of God connect to metaphysics (B.1), philosophy of religion (B.14), and natural theology.
Atheism engages with the philosophy of religion (B.14) and metaphysical questions about God and existence.
Agnosticism connects to epistemology (B.3) and the philosophy of religion (B.14) as divisions of philosophy.
Humanism relates to philosophical anthropology (B.5), ethics (B.6), and the philosophy of religion (B.14).
Secularism connects to political philosophy (B.7) and the philosophy of religion (B.14) as divisions of philosophy.
